title: Discipleship 11: Discipleship in the Family — Children in Public Worship
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Discipleship 11 - Discipleship in the Family - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Why Children Should Be in Corporate Worship
subpoints: 56 children under age 12 in the congregation; 19 under age 5; 40.6% of regular attendees are under 12
This is not a problem — these are the voices of the gospel going forth to the next generation
The question: why do we want young children in the worship service, and why is it so difficult?
This lesson is for the whole church, not just parents — all have made covenantal vows at baptism to minister to covenant children

outline.title: Biblical Basis: Children Have Always Been Present in Worship
subpoints: The visible church was established with Abraham, including children, and with a sacrament (circumcision) — children are part of the visible church
Deuteronomy 29:10-12: covenant ceremony includes 'your little ones and your wives'
Deuteronomy 31:12-13: gather the people — men, women, little ones — to hear the Word every seven years
Joshua 8:35: Joshua read the whole law to 'all the assembly of Israel, with the women, the little ones, and the strangers'
Nehemiah 12:43: children rejoice loudly enough to be heard far away at the end of a half-day worship service
Joel 2:15-16: the eschatological worship service includes nursing babes — the church in the age of the Messiah includes its youngest members

outline.title: Jesus Desires Children to Come to Him in Worship
subpoints: The entire gospel narrative is bracketed by children worshiping Christ: John in the womb (Luke 1:44) and children on Palm Sunday (Matthew 21:15-16)
Mark 10:13-16: parents bring infants to be blessed; the disciples try to keep them away; Jesus is indignant
These are covenant children — Jesus is in Judea, among the covenant people of God; their parents' desire to bring them confirms it
Verse 14: these children are part of the kingdom of God; verse 15: the command for them to come is expressed through parents
Hebrews 2:12: Jesus praises God 'in the midst of the assembly' — he meets especially with his people in the gathered congregation
The Westminster Confession: the visible church consists of all who profess the true religion together with their children

outline.title: Why Is This So Difficult? Three Failures of Faith
subpoints: Children are sinners; parents are also sinners and don't want the work
We lack faith in what the Bible says concerning the importance of corporate worship: do we truly believe being here is the best possible use of this time?
We lack faith in what the Bible says concerning preaching and regeneration: the argument 'they can't understand' misunderstands how regeneration works
1 Corinthians 2:14: the natural man cannot receive the things of the Spirit — understanding follows regeneration, not the other way around
Ephesians 2:1: the preached Word is spoken to dead people; Ezekiel 37 shows God using the preached Word to give life to dry bones
Satan is actively opposed to the whole church of Christ — including children — worshiping together; he discourages it because he knows the damage it does to his kingdom

outline.title: Practical Guidance for Parents and the Whole Congregation
subpoints: Right belief must lead to right practice: God wants young children in worship; the preached Word is powerful regardless of the child's cognitive ability
Training children for worship begins on Monday, not Sunday; if they don't obey at home, they won't obey in church
For parents: prepare children before the service; explain what is happening during each element; bring them back after discipline outside, not out for the whole service
For those without young children: be patient; pray specifically for the children; sit near families with small children to help; do not distract children with faces or waving
We are the body of Christ and we worship as one — no age group is excluded

summary:
This session addresses the place of young children in the public corporate worship of the Lord on the Lord's Day. With 56 children under age 12 making up over 40% of the congregation's regular attendees, the question is not hypothetical. Pastor Lyro makes the case from Scripture that the presence of children in worship is not a modern innovation to be tolerated but a biblical expectation rooted in the nature of the covenant community itself.

The biblical case begins with the Abrahamic covenant, which established the visible church with children explicitly included. It runs through Deuteronomy 29 and 31 (children commanded to be present at covenant ceremonies and the public reading of the law), Joshua 8 and Nehemiah 12 (all-age worship in historical practice), and Joel 2 (nursing babes present in the eschatological gathering of the Messiah's church). The gospel accounts complete the picture: Christ's earthly ministry is bracketed by children worshiping him, and Mark 10:13-16 records his indignation at the disciples who tried to keep covenant children away from him, commanding that they be brought to him because they are part of the kingdom of God.

The session then honestly confronts why having children in worship is difficult. Three failures of faith are identified: we do not truly believe that corporate worship is the most important thing we can do on the Lord's Day; we do not believe what the Bible says about preaching and regeneration (the argument that children cannot benefit because they cannot understand misunderstands the doctrine of the new birth, which precedes and enables understanding); and Satan actively opposes the intergenerational worship of the whole body of Christ because of the damage it does to his kingdom.

The lesson closes with practical guidance for parents — training begins at home all week long, not Sunday morning — and for the broader congregation, who are called to be patient with, pray for, and actively support families with young children rather than treating them as a nuisance.
transcript:
## Why Children Belong in Corporate Worship

We continue our series on discipleship in the family, moving now to the important question of children in the public corporate worship of the Lord. This is relevant not only for parents of young children but for all of us. Our covenant baptism services conclude with a charge to the entire congregation: this child is committed to you for your loving care, as God is your judge. Children's worship is a whole-church matter.

## Does the Bible Teach That Children Should Be in the Worship Service?

Before anything else, we must ask what Scripture says. If the Bible does not include children in public worship, the discussion is over. But in fact the Bible's testimony is consistent and clear.

**The covenant with Abraham** (Genesis 17) established the visible church with a promise and a sacrament that explicitly included children. The circumcision of male children on the eighth day was the sign of inclusion in God's covenant community. Children have been part of the visible church since its institution.

**The worship assemblies of the Old Covenant** commanded children's presence. In Deuteronomy 29, Moses addresses "all of you" — leaders, elders, men, little ones, women — in a covenant-making assembly. In Deuteronomy 31, God commands that when all Israel comes to appear before the Lord at the Feast of Tabernacles, the entire book of Deuteronomy is to be read aloud: *"Gather the people together, men and women and little ones, and the stranger who is within your gates, that they may hear, that they may learn to fear the Lord your God."*

**Joshua read the whole law** before the entire assembly: *"There was not a word of all that Moses had commanded which Joshua did not read before all the assembly of Israel, with the women, the little ones, and the strangers who were living among them."* (Joshua 8:35)

**The prophet Joel**, describing the worship of the messianic age that began with Christ's resurrection, includes nursing infants in the solemn assembly (Joel 2:16). The whole church, from oldest to youngest, is summoned to worship.

**In the New Testament**, Paul's letters to the Ephesians and Colossians address children directly as part of the gathered congregation. These letters were read aloud in the church service. Paul expected children to be present and to hear themselves addressed. This means the children are not an afterthought; they are members of the assembly.

**Jesus's interaction with children** during his ministry brackets his entire earthly life: John the Baptist leaped for joy in the womb at the approach of Mary (Luke 1); and children cried "Hosanna!" as Jesus entered Jerusalem (Matthew 21:15–16). When the disciples tried to keep children from him, Jesus was indignant and said:

> *"Let the little children come to Me, and do not forbid them; for of such is the kingdom of God."* (Mark 10:14)

In Mark 10:13–16, parents were bringing infants to Jesus for him to touch and bless. The disciples tried to stop them. Jesus was *angry* — one of the very few times Scripture uses that word about him — and commanded that the children be brought to him. He took them in his arms and blessed them, declaring that of such is the kingdom of God. Jesus meets especially with his people in the congregation of the assembly.

## Why Is It So Difficult?

If God has always included children in the corporate worship of his people, why do we find it so hard?

**Our children are sinners.** They will act like it. This is simply true and must be acknowledged.

**We are sinners and selfish.** We do not want to do the hard work of training them.

**We lack faith in what the Bible says about worship.** Do we really believe that being in the house of God on the Lord's Day is the most important thing we can be doing? The fruit of our choices suggests that many of us — myself included at times — do not truly believe this.

**We lack faith in what the Bible says about preaching and regeneration.** The most common argument I hear against having children in the worship service is: "They can't understand anything." But regeneration has nothing to do with cognitive ability. Paul writes: *"The natural man does not receive the things of the Spirit of God, for they are foolishness to him; nor can he know them, because they are spiritually discerned."* (1 Corinthians 2:14) We were all in that condition once. God did not wait until we could understand before giving us new hearts. He gave us new hearts so that we could understand. The preaching of the word is the very means God uses to give life to the dead — as Ezekiel 37 dramatically illustrates in the valley of dry bones: the prophet preached to dead, dry bones, and through the preaching the Spirit gave them life. Can God not do that for your child through the preaching of the word?

**We do not take the time to train our children for worship.** Training children to sit in the worship service does not begin on Sunday — it begins on Monday. If we do not expect them to sit and listen during family devotions at home, we cannot expect it here. Training starts at home.

**Satan knows the damage that true worship does to his kingdom.** He is deeply invested in keeping children out of the sanctuary.

## How Do We Improve?

**Right belief.** Whatever God says, we do. If God wants young children in his worship service — and he does — then we worship as a whole body.

**Right love.** It is not all about us. Those without young children: be patient with those who are training little ones. Do not offer self-righteous judgment. Pray for them specifically. Offer to sit near families and help guide children gently when needed. Do not make faces at the children during the service — that works against everything their parents are trying to do.

Those with young children: prepare your children before the service. Walk them through what will happen. Discipline them during the service so that those around you are not unduly distracted. If a child is crying and cannot be controlled, take them out briefly, address their hearts, and bring them back. Training in worship begins at home and extends into the service.

**Right focus.** We are the body of Christ. The children are members of that body. We come before God together — the oldest and the youngest, because all belong to him.

> *"We will not hide them from their children, telling to the generation to come the praises of the Lord, and His strength and His wonderful works that He has done."* (Psalm 78:4)

That is what we are trying to do as a church: hand the faith to the next generation by including them in the worship of the living God.
